entries.description: Persistent practice and dispassion, the means by which the restless mind is brought under control.
The yoga of repeated practice; disciplined, sustained effort to steady the mind on the divine.
The imperishable state or station, attained by the wise who renounce the fruits of action.
Unrighteousness, the opposite of dharma; a confused intellect mistakes it for dharma.
The principle governing the elements/material beings; one of the aspects in which Krishna is realized at death.
The principle presiding over the gods/cosmic forces; an aspect in which Krishna is to be known.
The bodily seat or basis, listed first among the five causes of action.
The principle presiding over sacrifice (yajña); those who see Krishna in it know him even at the moment of departure.
The principle pertaining to the self; known fully by those who strive to overcome death.
The Adityas, a class of solar deities; Krishna says among them he is Vishnu.
The fire deity; Krishna says among the Vasus he is Agni.
Ego or the sense of 'I'; listed among the causes of action and as an obstacle to be relinquished.
The ego or sense of 'I'; one of the constituents enumerated as part of the field.
Non-injury or non-violence; listed among the components of bodily austerity.
Indra's elephant; Krishna says among elephants he is Airavata.
Unborn; an attribute of Krishna's eternal nature that the deluded cannot perceive.
The letter/sound 'a'; Krishna says among letters he is the akāra.
Inaction or non-action; the wise see inaction within action and action within inaction.
Space or sky; likened to the all-pervading container of beings.
The imperishable; the unchanging one, the second of the two puruṣas in the world.
The conch of Yudhishthira.
Single-minded, undivided devotion to the one Lord.
Detached or unattached; the stance by which actions do not bind the Lord.
The downward/outgoing vital breath, offered together with prana in the practice of breath control.
The lower (material) nature of Krishna, made of the eight elements.
The Pandava prince to whom Krishna speaks; he asks Krishna to describe his manifestations.
Offering or dedication; the act of consecrating everything to the Lord.
Chief of the ancestral spirits (pitris); Krishna identifies himself with him.
A firm seat, neither too high nor too low, set in a clean place for meditation.
The cosmic peepal/fig tree, here imagined with roots above and branches below, symbolizing the world of existence (saṁsāra).
Son of Drona and a warrior fighting for the Kauravas.
The twin Ashvin deities, physicians of the gods, seen within the cosmic form.
The sage Asita, named alongside Devala among the rishis who praise Krishna.
Demonic in nature; the faith of those who practice harsh austerities out of pride and torment themselves and the self within.
The 'demoniac endowment' — qualities like hypocrisy, arrogance, anger and cruelty that lead toward bondage and lower births.
The self or soul that abides in the body without acting or being tainted; sought through meditation and self-restraint.
Knowledge of the self, which dispels ignorance and reveals the supreme reality.
Knowledge of the self, the teaching by which Arjuna says his delusion was removed.
The self or soul; here those steadfast in the self (ātma-niṣṭha) attain the eternal state.
Judging others by analogy with oneself; seeing others' pleasure and pain as one's own marks the highest yogi.
The unmanifest root of nature from which the constituents of the field arise.
Krishna, who is teaching Arjuna; in this chapter he describes his divine manifestations (vibhūti). The speaker of most verses here.
A devotee; the Lord declares his devotee never perishes.
Devotion to Krishna; the devotee who realizes this knowledge attains union with him.
The path of loving devotion, by which one worships the divine with single-minded focus.
The sustainer or supporter; an epithet of the Lord.
States or dispositions arising from the three guṇas, by which the world is deluded.
Second of the Pandava brothers, renowned for strength; he protects the Pandava army and blows the conch Paundra.
The elder Kuru commander on the Kaurava side, among the warriors already doomed in the cosmic vision.
The enjoyer or recipient; the Lord as the one who receives all sacrifices.
A sage; Krishna says among the maharshis he is Bhrigu.
The elements or beings; the five gross elements among the constituents of the field, and creatures in general.
Epithet of Krishna meaning 'source/originator of beings'; vocative used by Arjuna.
Epithet of Krishna meaning 'lord of beings'; vocative used by Arjuna.
The seed which the Lord, as father, sows in the field of Nature to produce all beings.
The ultimate, beginningless reality; the absolute that is neither existent nor non-existent in ordinary terms.
The path to brahman; Arjuna fears that one bewildered upon it may perish like a scattered cloud.
Extinction or merging in Brahman; the state of peace attained by the self-realized yogi.
The discipline of celibacy and study through which seekers pursue the supreme goal.
Brahma the creator, whose single day lasts a thousand yugas and whose night is of equal length.
The worlds beginning with Brahma's realm, from which beings still return to rebirth until they reach the divine.
The supreme reality/absolute; Arjuna addresses Krishna as the supreme Brahman.
The first varna; its natural duties include calm, restraint, austerity, purity, and knowledge.
The boundless bliss of brahman, enjoyed by the yogi who has united with the self and burned away faults.
Sages of the brahmin order; their attainment through devotion is taken for granted.
The knower of Brahman, for whom all the Vedas have the limited use that a well has when water is everywhere.
The state established in Brahman, the condition of one who has attained spiritual realization.
Preceptor of the gods; Krishna says among priests he is Brihaspati.
A chant of the Sama-veda; Krishna says among the Sama hymns he is the Brihat-saman.
The intellect or faculty of discernment; listed among the elements of the field.
The accumulated mental impressions of intellect from a previous life, which the reborn yogi reconnects with to resume his striving.
The discus weapon held by Krishna in his four-armed form.
An outcaste; listed with a learned brahmin, a cow, an elephant and a dog as beings the self-knower regards with equal vision.
The moon deity; Krishna says among the stars he is the moon.
The realm of the moon reached by those who take the dark path; from there beings return to worldly existence.
The moving and unmoving, i.e. all animate and inanimate creation.
A warrior allied with the Pandavas.
King of the Gandharvas; Krishna says among Gandharvas he is Chitraratha.
A class of titans/antagonists of the gods; Krishna says among them he is Prahlada.
Fate or destiny, named as the fifth of the causes of any action.
Deities; those who worship other gods are said to worship the Lord, but indirectly.
Divine; describing Krishna's three-guṇa māyā.
The 'divine endowment' — qualities like fearlessness, purity, nonviolence and self-control that lead toward liberation.
The southern course of the sun; the dark path (smoke, night, waning fortnight) by which the departed reach the moon-realm and return to rebirth.
Hypocrisy or ostentation; acts done for show, characterizing rajas and asuri practice.
Charity or giving, listed with sacrifice and austerity as acts to be performed without attachment.
A class of demons; named among beings who cannot know Krishna's true form.
Kusha grass spread, along with a hide and cloth, to form the seat for meditation.
Gods or deities; worshippers of gods attain the gods.
Epithet of Krishna meaning 'god of gods'; vocative used by Arjuna.
The conch of Arjuna.
The sage Devala, named with Asita among the rishis who praise Krishna.
Celestial sages; Krishna says among them he is Narada.
The supreme abode of the Lord, self-luminous and not lit by sun, fire, or moon, from which one does not return.
Epithet of Arjuna ('winner of wealth'); Krishna says he is Dhananjaya among the Pandavas.
Epithet meaning bow-wielder, applied to Arjuna in the final verse.
Right conduct/moral law; Krishna says of those who seek victory he is their dharma (just policy).
The essence of dharma or righteous duty, which the devout pursue with faith.
An epithet of Yudhishthira, the eldest Pandava.
A righteous soul; the wayward devotee soon becomes one and gains lasting peace.
A war fought in accordance with righteousness; held to be a duty for the warrior class.
King of Chedi, a warrior on the Pandava side.
The king Dhritarashtra, addressed by Sanjaya as he narrates the events.
The sons of Dhritarashtra, the Kaurava warriors, whom Arjuna sees rushing into Krishna's mouths.
Steadfastness or resolve, classified into sattvic, rajasic, and tamasic kinds.
The discipline of meditation in which the seeker becomes absorbed.
The divine sight Krishna grants Arjuna so he can behold the cosmic form, which ordinary eyes cannot see.
Daughter of Drupada and wife of the Pandavas; her sons fight on the Pandava side.
Material sacrifice using physical offerings, ranked below the sacrifice of knowledge.
The teacher of military arts to the Pandavas and Kauravas, fighting for the Kauravas.
King of Panchala, father of Draupadi and Dhrishtadyumna, a warrior on the Pandava side.
"Son of Drupada," i.e. Dhrishtadyumna, the commander who arrayed the Pandava army.
An evildoer; even such a one, if devoted single-mindedly, is to be regarded as virtuous.
Eldest son of Dhritarashtra and leader of the Kaurava forces.
The dvandva (copulative) compound; Krishna says among grammatical compounds he is the dvandva.
A "twice-born" person learned and humble; cited as one whom the wise see equally with all other beings.
The single syllable (Om); Krishna says among utterances he is the one syllable, and among letters he is 'a' (akāra).
The mace carried by Krishna in his four-armed form, which Arjuna asks him to take up again.
Celestial musicians; Krishna says among Gandharvas he is Chitraratha.
Arjuna's bow, which slips from his hand as his resolve weakens.
The river Ganga; Krishna says among rivers he is the Ganga.
The Gayatri metre/mantra; Krishna says among metres he is the Gayatri.
The qualities or strands of nature (prakṛti) that bind the embodied self and determine its rebirths.
Name of Vishnu/Krishna; Sanjaya recalls Hari's form with wonder.
The oblation offered into the sacrificial fire.
The Himalaya range; Krishna says among immovable things he is Himalaya.
Offering oblations into fire; an act to be dedicated to the Lord.
Epithet of Krishna meaning lord of the senses.
The king to whom Manu transmitted the teaching of yoga, continuing the ancient lineage.
King of the gods; Krishna says among the gods he is Indra.
The senses; the eleven sense-faculties counted among the elements of the field.
An epithet of Krishna meaning lord of the world.
Epithet of Krishna meaning 'lord of the world'; vocative used by Arjuna.
King Janaka and others like him; cited as examples of those who attained perfection through action ('जनकादिक' = Janaka and the rest).
Epithet of Krishna; used by Arjuna while addressing him about the yoga of equanimity.
Old age and death; the devotees take refuge in Krishna to overcome them.
A Kaurava-side king named among the warriors Krishna declares already destroyed.
The seeker of knowledge; one of the four kinds of devotees who worship Krishna.
One who has conquered the self, remaining peaceful and seeing brahman alone amid honor and dishonor, heat and cold, pleasure and pain.
The eternal individual living being, here described as an eternal portion (aṁśa) of the Lord in the world.
Knowledge; here the discernment of the difference between the field and the knower of the field.
The vision born of knowledge, attainable through exclusive devotion, leading to entry into Krishna.
Knowledge and realized/experiential knowledge; the yogi content with both views gold, stone and clay alike.
The sacrifice of knowledge; one who studies this dialogue is said to worship Krishna by it.
The fire of knowledge, which reduces all actions to ashes as a kindled fire turns wood to ash.
One possessed of knowledge or wisdom; here, a knower devoted to the welfare of all beings.
That which is to be known — the supreme reality, knowing which one attains immortality.
The state of liberation/absolute freedom attained by acting without attachment.
Time, which Krishna declares himself to be, the destroyer of worlds, grown ready to consume the people.
A cosmic age; at its end (kalpānta) beings dissolve and at its start (kalpārambha) they are awakened again.
Desire; Krishna says he is desire (kāma) that is not contrary to dharma, the cause of generation.
The wish-fulfilling cow; here a metaphor for sacrifice as the granter of all desires.
Desire and anger; mastering their urges is essential to becoming a true yogi.
Those deluded by desire who pursue heaven and remain caught in coming and going.
The wish-fulfilling cow; Krishna says among cows he is Kamadhenu.
The monkey-bannered chariot of Arjuna, named for the figure of Hanuman on its flag.
The sage Kapila, founder of Samkhya; Krishna says among the perfected ones (siddhas) he is Kapila.
Action or work; the chapter analyzes its causes, fruits, and threefold qualitative types.
The path of disciplined action by which others realize the self.
A warrior allied with the Kauravas, named among those Krishna says are already slain.
The king of Kashi (Varanasi), an archer fighting for the Pandavas.
The sons of Dhritarashtra and their army, the side led by Duryodhana.
Epithet of Krishna, addressed by Arjuna.
The crown worn by Krishna in his four-armed form, which Arjuna wishes to see again.
Singing the praises of the Lord as a form of worship.
Kripacharya, a teacher and warrior on the Kaurava side.
Arjuna's charioteer and teacher who speaks the Gita's teaching; also appears in the compound कृष्णार्जुन. Addressed by Sanjaya as योगेश्वर (lord of yoga).
The dark half of the lunar month, part of the path leading back to rebirth.
Anger; arising together with desire from the rajas quality, named as an enemy.
The perishable; the term for all changeable beings, one of the two puruṣas in the world.
The duties natural to the kshatriya (warrior) class: valor, firmness, protecting people, generosity, and leadership.
The warrior-ruler class whose duty includes righteous combat.
The 'field' — the body and its constituents, the object of knowledge as opposed to the knower.
The 'knower of the field' — the conscious self or spirit that dwells in the body; identified by Krishna with himself.
Lord of wealth and the Yakshas; Krishna identifies himself with him among Yakshas and Rakshasas.
The hereditary duties and customs of a family or lineage.
A destroyer of one's own family or lineage.
The two Kumaras, mind-born ascetic sons, seen within the cosmic form.
King Kuntibhoja (or his kin), an ally on the Pandava side.
The field where the Kaurava and Pandava armies assembled for battle; the setting of the Gita.
The welfare and holding-together of the world; the purpose for which the wise should continue to act.
An epithet of Krishna, used by Arjuna when the milder human form returns.
A great chariot-warrior capable of fighting many opponents at once.
Great sages; including the seven ancient seers said to have arisen from Krishna's will.
Great-souled persons who worship the Lord with single-minded devotion.
The great wind that abides everywhere in space, used as an image for how beings rest in the Lord.
The mind, said to be higher than the senses but lower than the intellect.
The human form in which the Lord is seen, which the deluded undervalue.
The conch of Sahadeva.
Sacred verse or formula used in ritual; identified with the Lord.
The progenitor figures (the four Manus) who came from Krishna's will, from whom worldly creatures descend.
A month of the Hindu calendar (roughly Nov–Dec); Krishna says among months he is Margashirsha.
Marichi, regarded as chief of the winds (Maruts); Krishna identifies himself with him.
Action performed for the sake of the divine; work done as service to Krishna.
Being wholly devoted to the divine, holding Krishna as the supreme goal.
Being wholly devoted to and absorbed in Krishna, with the mind fixed on him.
The power by which the supreme lord moves all beings as if set upon a machine.
Mount Meru, the mountain of cosmology; Krishna says among lofty things he is Meru.
A hypocrite or false-conduct person who restrains the senses outwardly while dwelling on objects of enjoyment in the mind.
Delusion; abandoning duty out of it is tamasic, and Arjuna declares his delusion finally gone.
Liberation, attained by devotion to one's own duty and worship of the source of all beings.
The abode/state of liberation; Arjuna addresses Krishna as the goal of mokṣa.
The abode of liberation, of which Arjuna says Krishna alone has knowledge.
Death; the Lord declares himself to be death as well as immortality (mokṣa). Mr̥tyu-loka is the mortal world.
Seekers of liberation, who in the past performed action knowing this truth; Arjuna is urged to follow their example.
Sages or contemplative ascetics who attained liberation through this knowledge.
Serpent beings; Krishna says among nagas he is Shesha.
The state of actionlessness in action, attained by one free of attachment and self-mastery.
Fourth Pandava brother; he blows the conch Sughosha.
A celestial sage (devarshi); Krishna says among divine sages he is Narada.
Hell; lust, anger and greed are called its three gates.
A disbeliever or one who lacks faith in the teaching.
The "city of nine gates", a metaphor for the human body in which the embodied self dwells.
Restraint or control of the mind, which Arjuna deems difficult and Krishna says is achieved through practice and detachment.
Quality-less; describing the supreme self that experiences the guṇas yet remains untouched by them.
Free from desire; the desireless strength that Krishna is in the strong.
Firm establishment or steadfastness, here the highest standing in knowledge.
The sacred syllable Om, identified by Krishna as the sound (śabda) in the Vedas.
The threefold designation of Brahman — Om, Tat and Sat — uttered in connection with sacrifice, gift and austerity; Sat also denotes truth, goodness and right action.
The syllable Om, identified with the Lord as the worthy object of knowledge.
A lotus leaf, used as a simile: one who acts surrendering to Brahman is untouched by sin as a lotus leaf is untouched by water.
The conch of Krishna.
The sons of Pandu; Krishna says among the Pandavas he is Dhananjaya (Arjuna).
A wise or learned person; one whose actions are burned by knowledge and who acts free of desire-driven intentions.
Father of the five Pandava brothers and brother of Dhritarashtra.
Those born of lowly or sinful origin, who too attain liberation through devotion.
The higher nature of Krishna, the life-principle (jīva) that upholds the whole world.
Another's duty; following it is said to be fearsome compared to one's own dharma.
The supreme self or ultimate reality; the goal to be sought from which there is no return.
The principle of the supreme self, which Arjuna recognizes Krishna to be.
The supreme lord said to dwell in the heart of all beings, turning them as if mounted on a machine.
Possessiveness or accumulation; the restrained one acts with the body alone, having given up desire and possession.
Epithet of Arjuna, meaning son of Pritha (Kunti); used by Sanjaya in the closing verse.
The conch blown by Bhima.
The bestower of the fruits of action and sacrifice.
Renunciation of the fruits of one's actions, which is said to bring peace.
Ancestors or departed forefathers; their worshippers attain them.
The ancestral spirits; Krishna says among them he is Aryama.
The ancestral deities (Pitris) seen within the cosmic form.
The Lord (Krishna), who, like the wind carrying scent, takes the senses and mind from one body to another.
A devotee-prince born among the daityas; Krishna says among the daityas he is Prahlada.
The progenitor or lord of creatures, addressed as forefather within the cosmic form.
The wise or discerning one who, free of delusion and attachment, enters the eternal state.
Primordial nature or material substance, from which the jīva draws the senses and mind.
Wisdom or discerning intellect; described as becoming steady (sthira) in the realized person.
The vital breath; some offer the in-breath into the out-breath, or restrain breath and senses as sacrifice.
The vital breaths prāṇa (outgoing) and apāna (downward), regulated to digest food in the body.
Breath control; achieved by restraining the movement of the in-breath (prana) and out-breath (apana).
Departure, i.e. the moment of death; a key theme of this chapter on remembering the divine at the final passage.
Religious merit, which earns heaven but is exhausted, returning one to the mortal world.
The worlds of merit where one who falls from yoga dwells before being reborn into a favorable family.
A warrior of the Kunti-Bhoja line fighting for the Pandavas.
The conscious self or spirit that experiences the qualities of nature; the enjoyer paired with prakṛti.
Human striving or capacity for effort; Krishna identifies himself as this in people.
The Supreme Person, transcending both the perishable and imperishable; the highest reality identified with the Lord himself.
Attraction and aversion; the pair from which the delusion of dualities arises in the mind.
The 'royal knowledge' or foremost of sciences, the central teaching of this chapter.
Royal sages, kings who became seers; likewise attain the Lord through devotion.
The quality of passion and restless activity among the three guṇas, marked by desire and attachment to fruit.
The quality of passion/activity (rajas), one of the three guṇas, from which desire and anger arise.
A class of malevolent demon-beings; mentioned among the objects worshipped by those of rajas temperament.
Rama, named among warriors who bear weapons; Krishna identifies himself with him.
Sages/seers who praise Krishna with one voice.
The Rudras, deities associated with Shiva; Krishna says among them he is Sadashiva.
A form of Shiva; Krishna says among the Rudras he is Sadashiva.
A spiritual aspirant who binds the mind, gives up craving and unites with the self in solitude.
A virtuous or righteous person.
A class of celestial beings (the Sadhyas) seen within the cosmic form.
The ocean; Krishna says among bodies of water he is the ocean.
Youngest Pandava brother; he blows the conch Manipushpaka.
The witness; the Lord as detached observer overseeing nature's workings.
The state of meditative absorption, kindled inwardly through contemplation as a form of inner sacrifice.
Equanimity or evenness of mind toward pleasure and pain, success and failure; called the essence of yoga.
The cycle of worldly existence and repeated birth and death.
The ocean of worldly existence and rebirth, from which Krishna promises to rescue his devotees.
Doubt; the doubting person finds happiness in neither this world nor the next, and is told to cut doubt with the sword of knowledge.
The yoga of equanimity or seeing all alike, which Arjuna says is hard to sustain because of the restless mind.
Self-restraint; the self-controlled (samyami) perform sacrifice, and some offer the senses into the fire of restraint (samyamagni).
Eternal, everlasting; describing the primal seed of all beings and Krishna's timeless nature.
The eternal essence of dharma; the timeless moral and spiritual order.
Narrator who recounts the Krishna–Arjuna dialogue to King Dhritarashtra; speaks the closing verses.
Resolve or intention; named by the Lord as one of his identities.
Confusion/intermixture (of duties and order); Krishna says that by ceasing to act he would cause such disorder and ruin.
The path of knowledge/analysis, contrasted here with the yoga of action.
The path of knowledge and analytical discrimination by which some perceive the self within.
Renunciation; here defined as giving up desire-driven (kāmya) actions. A संन्यासी is the renunciate.
The quality of purity and clarity among the three guṇas; actions, knowledge, intellect, etc. of this type are deemed highest.
Pertaining to sattva, the quality of purity and balance; the highest of the three modes of faith, food, sacrifice, austerity and gift.
Pertaining to sattva; the three modes (sattvika, rajasika, tamasika) of states/feelings arising from Krishna.
A Yadava warrior allied with the Pandavas.
"Son of Subhadra," i.e. Abhimanyu, son of Arjuna and a warrior on the Pandava side.
"Son of Somadatta," i.e. Bhurishrava, a warrior on the Kaurava side.
An epithet of Arjuna meaning one who can shoot the bow with either hand.
Union with or absorption into the supreme; the goal attained by the realized devotee.
A king allied with the Pandavas, described as foremost among men.
A conch shell blown as a war trumpet to signal the start of battle.
Surrender or taking refuge in God; attained after many births by the rare realized one.
The eternal moral order, which Arjuna says Krishna preserves.
The teaching or doctrine; here Krishna calls this most secret teaching, knowing which one becomes accomplished.
The cosmic serpent Shesha (Ananta); Krishna says among the nagas he is Shesha.
A Panchala warrior on the Pandava side, key in the defeat of Bhishma.
Faith or trust; the chapter explains it as threefold according to one's innate nature, and acts done without it (aśraddhā) are called fruitless.
Title for Krishna as the speaker of the Gita; literally the Lord. Here he expounds the imperishable cosmic tree and the supreme person.
The varna whose natural work is service.
The bright half of the lunar month, part of the path leading to brahman.
Perfected beings; Krishna says among them he is the sage Kapila.
Spiritual attainment or perfection, reached through renunciation and discipline.
Kartikeya, war-god; Krishna says among military commanders he is Skanda.
The moon or the lunar nourishing essence; the Lord says he becomes soma to nourish plants with sap.
Creation or the created world that revolves through nature's cycle.
One of steady wisdom; the person whose intellect is firm and unshaken amid all circumstances.
The conch of Nakula.
The sun deity; Krishna says among luminaries he is the sun.
One's own duty; said to be better even if imperfectly done than another's duty done well.
One's innate nature; people follow various observances according to their own nature.
One's own duty according to one's nature and station in life.
Regular self-study or recitation of scripture; counted as part of the austerity of speech.
Heaven, the realm of pleasures attained through merit, but temporary.
The quality of darkness, inertia, and delusion among the three guṇas, the lowest of the three types.
Austerity or disciplined effort, one of the purifying acts not to be given up.
Austerity or penance; one of the meritorious acts whose fruit the yogi transcends.
The knower of truth/reality, who is not entangled though the guṇas act upon the guṇas.
Reasoned discourse on truth/principles; Krishna says among debaters he is right argument (tatva-vada).
One who has transcended the three guṇas of Nature; the ideal state described in this chapter.
Relinquishment, defined as giving up the fruit of all action; classified into sattvic, rajasic, and tamasic kinds.
The horse that arose from the churning of the ocean of nectar; Krishna identifies himself with it among horses.
Ushanas (Shukra), seer-poet; Krishna says among poets/seers he is Ushanas.
A valiant warrior of Panchala fighting for the Pandavas.
The northern course of the sun; the bright path (fire, day, waxing fortnight) by which the departed reach brahman without return.
Detachment or dispassion, described as the weapon with which to cut down the deep-rooted tree of worldly existence.
The digestive fire residing in living beings; the Lord in this form digests food using the prāṇa and apāna breaths.
The varna whose natural work is farming, trade, and cattle-tending.
The thunderbolt weapon (of Indra); Krishna says among weapons he is the vajra.
The social-functional classes (brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya, shudra) whose duties are apportioned by innate qualities.
The intermixing or confusion of social classes, feared as a consequence of the breakdown of family order.
Deity of the waters; Krishna says among water-beings he is Varuna.
Desire or longing; Krishna is the desire in beings that does not transgress dharma.
The Vasus, a group of elemental deities; Krishna says among them he is Agni.
Name of Krishna (son of Vasudeva); Krishna says 'among the Vrishnis I am Vasudeva.'
King of the serpents; Krishna says among serpents he is Vasuki.
The wind deity, named among the powers present in and identified with the cosmic form.
The body of foundational scriptural knowledge; said to be the leaves of the cosmic tree and that which the Lord is ultimately to be known by.
Divine manifestation or glory of Krishna; the theme of this chapter, listing the forms in which his power is expressed.
The creator Brahma, seen seated in meditation on the lotus within the cosmic form.
Realized or applied knowledge, the experiential complement to jñāna.
Forbidden or wrong action, contrasted with ordinary action (karma) and inaction (akarma).
One of Dhritarashtra's sons, a warrior on the Kaurava side.
The cosmic, all-pervading form; Krishna calls himself the world-maker, the Virat (universal) self.
King of Matsya, an ally fighting for the Pandavas.
The deity Vishnu; Krishna says among the Adityas he is Vishnu.
Mover/director of the universe; an epithet of the Lord as its source and sustainer.
The universal or cosmic form of Krishna, encompassing all beings and worlds, revealed in this chapter.
The Vishvedevas, a group of universal deities, seen within the cosmic form.
Lord of the universe; an epithet of the supreme person who nourishes and pervades the world and is imperishable (avyaya).
Vows or observances kept rigorously by the self-controlled as part of their sacrificial discipline.
The Vrishni clan to which Krishna belongs; he says among the Vrishnis he is Vasudeva.
The manifest, perceptible form; the unwise take Krishna to be only this.
The sage Vyasa, traditional compiler of the Vedas and author of the Mahabharata; Krishna says among sages he is Vyasa.
Ritual sacrifice/offering; Krishna says among sacrifices he is japa (quiet recitation).
Nature-spirits (yakshas, paired with rakshas/demons); Krishna says among them he is Kubera.
God of death; Krishna says among controllers/restrainers he is Yama.
Spiritual discipline / union; here including buddhi-yoga, the yoga of discriminating intelligence by which Krishna draws devotees to himself.
One who has fallen from the path of yoga; after dwelling in higher worlds, such a one is reborn into a pure or yogic household.
Securing what one lacks and protecting what one has; the Lord guarantees this for steadfast devotees.
Krishna's veiling power; he remains hidden by it so the deluded do not recognize his unborn, eternal nature.
Perfection or accomplishment in yoga, which the one who strays from yoga fails to attain.
One established in yoga, who is unattached to actions and objects and has given up all resolves.
Epithet of Krishna meaning 'lord of yoga'; vocative used by Arjuna.
A practitioner of yoga; one disciplined in spiritual union. Here it refers to those devoted to either the personal or impersonal divine.
Womb or class of birth; here referring to the wombs/births into which those of demoniac nature are cast.
A valiant Panchala warrior on the Pandava side.
Eldest of the Pandava brothers; he blows the conch Anantavijaya.
A cosmic age; a thousand yugas make up a single day of Brahma.
